topic.what: Formal model showing that under governance-capability gap, capability improvements can paradoxically reduce optimal AI deployment in high-loss environments
topic.how: Joint optimization model of deployment α and security investment d under capability-damage bundling where authority exposure a(θ) links AI capability to breach consequences
topic.why_matters: Explains why firms may rationally deploy less capable AI despite productivity gains; governance maturity is prerequisite for capability improvements to translate into productive deployment

characteristics.what_it_measures: Organizational breadth of AI integration chosen by firm
characteristics.value: paradoxically lower in high-loss environments with capability-damage bundling
characteristics.evidence: Formal model: in high-loss environments (high λ), marginal security burden exceeds marginal productivity gain from capability improvement
characteristics.context: Result requires governance-capability gap where capability is bundled with authority exposure

characteristics.what_it_measures: Shortfall of optimal deployment below productivity-maximizing benchmark
characteristics.value: grows with breach-loss magnitude and capability level
characteristics.evidence: Model shows optimal deployment < productivity-maximizing level by security discount that widens with λ and θ
characteristics.context: Discount driven by capability-damage bundling under governance lag

characteristics.what_it_measures: Firm's chosen deployment level as function of AI capability θ
characteristics.value: non-monotonic: decreases then increases with capability in high-loss environments
characteristics.evidence: Paradox region where marginal security burden > marginal productivity gain; resolves when θ high enough for productivity to dominate
characteristics.context: Sign reversal requires: governance lag + high breach-loss environment + capability-authority co-variation

characteristics.what_it_measures: Conditional loss given compromise reflecting containment quality
characteristics.value: $4.88M average breach cost (IBM 2024); healthcare/financial sectors have higher λ
characteristics.evidence: Cited from IBM Security 2024; also CSA 2026 finding 97% lack proper AI access controls
characteristics.context: Treating λ as exogenous in baseline isolates capability effects; Section 3.2 endogenizes through governance investment

good_for: theoretical understanding of AI deployment constraints
identifying when governance maturity enables capability value capture
analyzing cyber risk implications of AI integration decisions
characterizing deployment thresholds under organizational risk
requires: governance-capability gap (controls not decoupled from capability)
capability-authority co-variation (more capable systems deployed with greater exposure)

novelty: Deployment paradox: AI capability improvements can reduce optimal deployment under governance lag in high-loss environments
Capability-damage bundling: formalizes how capability-linked authority exposure under governance lag increases conditional breach loss
Endogenous cyber risk: deployment choice jointly determines attack surface and breach consequences, creating feedback loop absent from prior models
Governance as condition: identifies governance investment as prerequisite for capability improvements to translate into productive deployment rather than security burden
open_problems: Empirical validation of deployment paradox in organizational settings with varying governance maturity
Conditions under which firms can decouple capability from authority exposure to eliminate bundling
Optimal governance investment strategies that shrink paradox region vs security investment tradeoffs
Social welfare implications and breach externalities expanding paradox beyond individual firm decisions
